segments.title: The 1801 precedent at Copenhagen
segments.summary: The opening recap explains how the 1801 battle put Copenhagen and the Danish fleet under British attack before the better-known 1807 return.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke begins with the first Battle of Copenhagen, naming Admiral Lord Parker and Lord Nelson as second in command. He presents the seizure of the Danish fleet at the capital as the decisive precedent for the later expedition. Starting here helps place 1807 in a pattern of repeated British coercion rather than as an isolated raid.

evidence.note: The transcript recaps Parker, Nelson, and the capture of the Danish fleet at Copenhagen.

segments.title: Britain returns for the Danish fleet in 1807
segments.summary: Admiral James Gambier's 1807 force returns to Copenhagen with army support and again takes the Danish fleet.
segments.body: Six years after the first attack, the Royal Navy returns under Admiral James Gambier with soldiers in support. The video notes Arthur Wellesley's presence and describes British troops engaging Danish forces away from Copenhagen, limiting their ability to move against the city. The core result is a second British seizure of the Danish fleet, which makes the episode an unusually stark example of naval power backed by a land operation.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Gambier's fleet, accompanying army forces, Wellesley, and the second capture of the Danish fleet.

segments.title: Why Britain repeated the Copenhagen attack
segments.summary: The closing comparison links the 1801 and 1807 fleet seizures to Britain's effort to keep Denmark from aligning with France.
segments.body: The video compresses the strategic rationale into a blunt warning: Britain did not want Denmark joining the French. By comparing 1801 with 1807, it emphasizes that control of a potentially hostile fleet mattered more than treating the Danish capital as a one-time target. This is a concise watch point on how alliance politics could drive coercive naval action.

evidence.note: The transcript compares 1801 and 1807 and states that Denmark should not join the French.
